sanskrit:
अथ चैनं नित्यजातं नित्यं वा मन्यसे मृतम्।
तथापि त्वं महाबाहो नैनं शोचितुमर्हसि॥
transliteration: atha cainaṃ nityajātaṃ nityaṃ vā manyase mṛtam | tathāpi tvaṃ mahābāho nainaṃ śocitum arhasi ||
literal_meaning: atha—if; ca—also; enam—this; nitya-jātam—always being born; nityam—forever; vā—or; manyase—you think; mṛtam—dead; tathāpi—even then; tvam—you; mahā-bāho—O mighty-armed; na—not; enam—this; śocitum—to lament; arhasi—should.
contextual_meaning: Krishna meets Arjuna at his own epistemic level: even if you adopt a strict materialist view that consciousness is only a temporary by-product of biology, grief is still irrational—chemicals are recycled every moment without sorrow.
psychological_state: Arjuna is caught between two world-views: a spiritual narrative he has inherited and a nascent materialist doubt that makes his guilt feel simultaneously real and absurd.
trigger_scenario: A hospice nurse who intellectually accepts that death is neuronal shutdown yet still feels overwhelming guilt after a patient codes under her watch.
anchor_text: Even if the self is just chemistry, no lasting thing is lost—grief is groundless.
